preFix.title: Needle-felted pig
preFix.subtitle: A rounded 10 cm pink merino pig with freestanding ears and a coiled tail
preFix.excerpt: A compact needle-felted pig in pink or rose merino roving. Egg-shaped body approximately 10 cm long, four short cylindrical legs, disc ears set at an angle, coiled tail, and a flat-disc snout with two indented nostrils. No armature required.
preFix.sourceNotes: Needle-felted animal figures are a standard project format in UK fibre arts teaching. Pig anatomy and construction approach synthesised from general needle felting practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

content.text: This pig is built from pink or rose merino
roving
without an
armature

. The body is an egg shape approximately 10 cm long, giving the pig its characteristic rounded belly-forward silhouette. Four short cylindrical legs hold it upright. The ears are thin felt discs needled on at a slight forward angle. The snout is a flattened disc with two small indented nostrils made with a single sharp
felting needle
pushed in and twisted. The tail is a tight coil of roving attached at the rump. Colours can be solid pink, mottled pink-and-cream, or spotted using the
inlay
technique.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

items.name: Merino roving, pink or rose, 19-21 micron
Merino roving, pale pink or cream, for snout and belly tones (optional)
Foam felting mat, at least 10 cm thick

content.text: Method
Step 1: Form the body

Pull a generous length of pink roving (approximately 30 cm). Roll it into a tight ball, then squeeze and reshape into an egg, wider at the belly end and narrower at the rump. The unfelted egg should be about 14 cm long (it will compress to 10 cm as you needle it). Place the egg on the foam mat and needle all over with the 38-gauge needle, rotating the piece constantly to felt evenly. Work inward from the outside surface. Continue until the body is firm, holds its shape when squeezed, and measures approximately 10 cm long.
Step 2: Make the legs

For each leg, pull a small length of roving and roll into a cylinder about 4 cm long and 2 cm in diameter. Needle on the mat until firm. Make four identical legs. To attach, hold a leg against the underside of the body in its intended position and needle through the leg into the body repeatedly until the join is solid. Test by holding the pig upright: the legs should not wobble. Adjust the position of each leg so the pig stands level before fully securing.
Step 3: Form and attach the snout

Roll a small amount of roving (about 4 g) into a flat disc roughly 3 cm in diameter and 1 cm thick. Needle the flat faces until firm but leave the circumference edge slightly loose. Press the disc against the widest end of the body (the snout end) and needle around the circumference to secure it. Once attached, use a single 38-gauge needle to push straight down twice at the centre of the snout disc to create two small nostril indentations. Push in about 5 mm, twist slightly, and withdraw.
Step 4: Make and attach the ears

For each ear, lay a thin wisp of roving flat on the foam mat and needle into a rounded triangular shape about 3 cm wide and 2.5 cm tall. Needle one face firmly, flip, needle the other face. Leave the bottom edge slightly loose: this is where the ear attaches to the head. Press an ear against the top of the head at a slight forward angle (tilted toward the snout) and needle the loose bottom edge into the body firmly. Repeat for the second ear, placing it symmetrically.
Step 5: Make and attach the tail

Pull a thin wisp of roving about 15 cm long. Needle it on the mat into a thin rope approximately 6 cm long and 5 mm in diameter. While slightly warm from handling, coil it into a tight spiral and needle the coils together lightly to hold the curl. Attach at the rump end of the body by needling the straight end of the tail into the body surface.
Step 6: Surface finish

Switch to the 40-gauge needle (or continue with 38-gauge) and work over the entire surface of the body with light, shallow strokes to smooth any loose fibres and even out the texture. Pay particular attention to the join areas around the legs, ears, snout, and tail to blend them into the body seamlessly.

items.fix: Add small amounts of roving to the front legs to lengthen them slightly and rebalance. In future, check standing balance at Step 2 before fully securing all four legs.
items.cause: The front legs are positioned too far back, or the belly-end of the body is too heavy
items.problem: The pig tips forward and does not stand upright

items.fix: Press the snout back into position and needle through the disc edge into the body at multiple angles until it is secure.
items.cause: Not enough needling through the circumference of the disc into the body
items.problem: The snout disc has detached or is loose

items.fix: Detach the ear gently and needle it more firmly on the mat until it holds its shape when held in the air. Then re-attach.
items.cause: The ear panels were not needled firmly enough before attachment

content.text: Care and finishing

Needle-felted figures are not washable in the usual sense: spot-clean with a barely damp cloth if dusty. Keep away from prolonged moisture. Display on a shelf or in a glass-topped box. Do not give to young children: needle felting produces loose fibres and the piece has no structural reinforcement for rough handling.
